1: Precise date: the ninth year of Zedekiah, tenth month (January 588 BCE). The Babylonian siege is documented in 2 Kings 25:1 and the Babylonian Chronicles. The specificity of the dating serves a theological function: YHWH's word spoken through Jeremiah is grounded in verifiable historical events, not general predictions. The beginning of the siege is the beginning of the end.
2: בֻּקְעָה הָעִיר (buq'ah ha'ir, the city was breached): baqa' (breach/split/cleave) is a violent word — a wall is torn open as a body is torn. The date: eleventh year of Zedekiah, fourth month, ninth day (July 586 BCE). The 18-month siege is over. Tisha B'Av (the ninth of Av) commemorates this event; the later temple destructions are dated to the same annual date in Jewish tradition, making it the day of national catastrophe.
3: The Babylonian officials take up their positions at the Middle Gate (sha'ar hatavekh). Their names and titles are given: Nergal-sharezer, Samgar-nebo, Sarsekim the Rab-saris, Nergal-sharezer the Rab-mag. The Rab-mag (rav mag) and Rab-saris (rav saris) are Babylonian administrative titles. The presence of named officials in a Hebrew text from this period provides historical corroboration of Jeremiah's setting.
4: וַיֵּצְאוּ לַיְלָה (vayyetze'u layelah, they went out by night): Zedekiah's midnight flight — the king who would not surrender by day flees by night. דֶּרֶךְ גַּן הַמֶּלֶךְ (derekh gan hammelekh, by way of the king's garden): the king who would not enter the way of covenant submission takes an escape route through his own garden. The irony of 'the king's garden' — all that worldly property he fled to preserve is left behind.
5: עֲרָבוֹת יְרִיחוֹ (aravot Yerikho, the plains of Jericho): the geography of the flight and capture is precise. מִשְׁפָּטִים (mishpatim, judgments): at Riblah before Nebuchadnezzar, Zedekiah faces formal legal judgments (mishpatim) — the covenant vocabulary for the legal verdict. Riblah in Syria is the administrative center where Nebuchadnezzar conducted his court.
6: אֶת-בְּנֵי צִדְקִיָּהוּ (et-benei Tzidkiyahu, the sons of Zedekiah): killed before their father's eyes. The fulfillment of 32:4 and 34:3 — Jeremiah had told Zedekiah he would see the king of Babylon face to face, and so he does, but as a prisoner watching his sons die. Also all the nobles of Judah (kol-chorei Yehudah) are executed — the leadership class that resisted YHWH's word is eliminated.
7: אֶת-עֵינֵי צִדְקִיָּהוּ עִוֵּר (et-einei Tzidkiyahu iver, he blinded the eyes of Zedekiah): the last thing Zedekiah sees is his sons' deaths; then his eyes are put out. The fulfillment of Ezek 12:13: 'I will bring him to Babylon ... yet he shall not see it' — a prophecy that seemed contradictory until now: he would be brought to Babylon but not see it (blinded before arrival). נְחֻשְׁתַּיִם (nechushtatayim, bronze chains — dual): the dual form = two fetters.
8: אֶת-בֵּית הַמֶּלֶךְ וְאֶת-בָּתֵּי הָעָם שָׂרַף (et-beit hammelekh ve'et-battei ha'am saraf, burned the king's palace and the people's houses): the covenant curse of Deut 28:52 realized — your cities will be besieged until your high and fortified walls come down. The burning of the physical city is the physical completion of the spiritual desolation that Jeremiah had been announcing for 40 years.
9: אֵת יֶתֶר הָעָם (et yeter ha'am, the rest of the people): Nebuzaradan the captain of the guard (rav tabachim, chief of the butchers/executioners — military title) exiles the survivors. The word for exile, golah, is from galah (to uncover, to go into exile, to strip bare) — the deportation as exposure and vulnerability.
10: דַּלַּת הָעָם (dallat ha'am, the poorest of the people — from dal, thin/weak/poor): the ironic remnant. Those who had nothing are given land and vineyards — the covenant promise of the land is partially fulfilled for the poor, the ones the ruling class always marginalized. The covenant's land-promise passes over the king and nobles to the dallim.
11: בְּיַד נְבוּזַרְאֲדָן (beyad Nevuzaradan, through Nebuzaradan): the divine arrangement is mediated through the foreign commander. YHWH's protection of Jeremiah reaches through the Babylonian chain of command — Nebuchadnezzar himself gives the orders.
12: קַח אֹתוֹ וְשִׂים עֵינֶיךָ עָלָיו (qach oto vesim einekha alav, take him and look after him): the 'set your eyes on him' idiom means attentive protection. כַּאֲשֶׁר יְדַבֵּר אֵלֶיךָ כֵּן עֲשֵׂה (ka'asher yedabber elekha ken aseh, do to him as he says to you): complete deference to the prophet's expressed wishes. The foreign king gives the prophet of YHWH more honor than the king of Judah did.
13: The named officials who carry out the orders — Nebuzaradan, Nebushazban the Rab-saris, Nergal-sharezer the Rab-mag — are the same officials from v3. The administrative machinery that conquered Jerusalem is now the instrument of Jeremiah's protection. Providence works through institutions, including hostile ones.
14: אֶל-גְּדַלְיָהוּ בֶּן-אֲחִיקָם בֶּן-שָׁפָן (el-Gedalyahu ben-Achiqam ben-Shaphan): Gedaliah son of Ahikam, the Shaphan-family member who had protected Jeremiah earlier (26:24). The family of Shaphan appears at every critical juncture of Jeremiah's protected survival. Jeremiah is handed over to the one family who consistently honored the prophetic word. לְהֵשֶׁב בְּתוֹךְ הָעָם (leheshev betokh ha'am, to live among the people): Jeremiah's preferred place — among the remaining people in the land.
15: A temporal insertion: the oracle to Ebed-melech was delivered while Jeremiah was still in the court of the guard — before the city fell. The placement here, after the fall narrative, signals the author's thematic intention: the faithfulness of the foreign servant is rewarded with a personal salvation oracle, placed in contrast to Zedekiah's fate.
16: מֵבִיא אֶת-דְּבָרַי אֶל-הָעִיר הַזֹּאת לְרָעָה וְלֹא לְטוֹבָה (mevi et-devarai el-ha'ir hazot lera'ah velo letovah, bringing my words against this city for harm and not for good): the words-coming-to-fulfillment formula. YHWH's words are the active agent — they 'come' (bo) to the city as he himself 'comes' in judgment. לְעֵינֶיךָ בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא (le'einekha bayyom hahu, before your eyes on that day): Ebed-melech will witness the fulfillment.
17: אֲבָל אַמַּלְטְךָ בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא (aval amaltetkha bayyom hahu, but I will deliver you on that day): the adversative aval (but/however) is the pivot of grace within judgment. While the city falls, the foreign servant is exempted. לֹא תִנָּתֵן בְּיַד הָאֲנָשִׁים אֲשֶׁר-אַתָּה יָגוֹר מִפְּנֵיהֶם (lo tinaten beyad ha'anashim asher-attah yagor mipneihem, you will not be given into the hands of the men you fear): the covenant protection language.
18: כִּי-בָטַחְתָּ בִּי (ki-vatachta bi, because you trusted in me): the theological ground of Ebed-melech's rescue — not his ethnicity, not his court status, but his batach (trust/reliance) in YHWH. The same verb (batach) used in the Psalms for confident trust in YHWH as refuge. A foreign servant who trusted YHWH receives the covenant promise of protection that Judah's own king refused through disobedience.
